reportTitle: AI Commercialization, Scaling, and Adoption: Strategies for Canadian Leadership
keyFindings: Canada has fallen from 6th to 47th place in the Digital Development Index since 2005, reflecting a systemic failure to translate world-class AI research into commercial and economic value.
Only 32.4% of Canadian-founded AI companies remain in Canada as of 2024, down from 74.9% in 2016, indicating a severe scale-up and talent retention crisis.

Canada's business sector lags behind G7 peers in AI digital intensity and adoption, with most SMEs — representing 99.8% of Canadian businesses — facing steep cost and knowledge barriers to AI deployment.
A 'valley of death' exists between early-stage AI research and market-ready products, driven by insufficient late-stage domestic capital, lack of anchor customers, and excessive procurement friction.

Ten of the twelve largest global tech companies are American; Canada has no global AI champion at the centacorn ($100B+) level, while the US dominates due to a self-reinforcing flywheel of capital, talent, and government procurement.
recommendations: Declare a National '1-10-100' Strategy: set targets of 1 centacorn, 10 decacorns, and 100 unicorns within 10 years; appoint a 10x Council and launch a Canadian Prosperity Fund (CPF) of at least $5B to invest in named Canadian Global Champions.

Reform SR&ED and IRAP: increase SR&ED reimbursement rates by at least 10 points, simplify applications, and refocus IRAP on market-validated AI companies rather than grant recipients; eliminate the cap-gains penalty on reinvestment into Canadian private companies.

Leverage government procurement as seed capital: establish 'Buy Canadian' mandates, publish AI procurement profiles, and require every federal department to become an early customer of Canadian AI solutions; deploy a Canadian AI Factory Program modelled on US SBIR/STTR.
Scale Creative Destruction Lab nationally and fund a research-to-seed pipeline; set commercialization KPIs for national AI institutes (MILA, Vector, AMII) alongside fundamental research metrics.

Build Canada's Sovereign AI Stack-on-Tap: invest in accessible, interoperable AI infrastructure (compute, data trusts, APIs) that enables rapid diffusion of AI capabilities across the entire economy and acts as a competitive grid for Canadian industry.

Declare Cohere Canada's national LLM champion and fuel it with large government revenue contracts; simultaneously pursue a Middle Powers Sovereign AI Coalition with aligned countries to pool capital and reduce dependence on US/China hyperscalers.

Run AI Moonshot programs in healthcare, education, natural resource management, and defence — with explicit outcome targets — to anchor domestic demand and establish reference deployments that drive global export opportunities.
policyGap: Canada lacks a coordinated national commercialization strategy that connects research output to market scale. Despite world-leading AI research, there is no sovereign wealth fund, no declared national AI champion, no reformed procurement fast-track, and no unified framework to convert publicly funded IP into domestic economic advantage — causing Canada to consistently pay for innovations that others monetize.

reportTitle: Sustaining and Scaling Canada's AI Research and Talent Advantage
keyFindings: Canada's foundational AI research leadership — born from the work of Hinton, Bengio, and LeCun — remains real but increasingly fragile as global compute investment and talent poaching accelerate; in 2024 the US produced 40 notable AI models, China 15, Europe 3, and Canada only 1.

University AI labs anchored by CIFAR-administered AI Chairs are Canada's primary talent factory, but the program is operating at capacity with insufficient per-chair funding, limiting lab sizes and supervisory bandwidth for graduate training.

Canada's research infrastructure — compute, data environments, and secure collaboration tools — has not scaled with global demand, forcing researchers to rely on foreign platforms and limiting Canada's ability to retain IP and spinouts domestically.

Over 110 stakeholder consultations identified a systemic gap between research funding and commercialization: most university R&D never reaches market, and embedded IP advisors and founders-in-residence are nearly absent from Canadian universities.

A lack of disciplinary focus means Canada's research effort is spread too thin; concentration in high-impact domains (health AI, robotics, scientific discovery) where Canada has comparative advantage would yield stronger global leadership.
recommendations: Expand the AI Chairs program by 40% and double the average award value per chair; allow additional 5-year renewals; maintain the world-class selection bar administered through CIFAR and the three national AI institutes (MILA, Vector, AMII).

Provide sustained, multi-year (10-year horizon) funding for MILA, Vector, and AMII tied to excellence metrics in both research and commercialization; launch DARPA-style national AI Grand Challenges in health, climate, energy, and security.

Stand up sovereign AI compute infrastructure and trusted data environments accessible to all Canadian researchers; formulate a Canadian semiconductor strategy to position domestic firms in the international supply chain.

Establish internationally recognized pre-competitive industrial R&D centres in focused domains (AI for health, AI for robotics, AI for scientific discovery) co-governed by academia, industry, and government.

Embed commercialization talent — IP advisors, tech-transfer specialists, founders-in-residence — within every major university AI lab to convert discoveries into spinouts before IP migrates abroad; create a national research-to-seed pipeline scaling CDL nationally.

Reform immigration for AI talent: create a fast-track 14-day approval stream for world-class AI researchers and entrepreneurs; fund a new multi-disciplinary Canada-AI Chairs program for international faculty recruitment; re-establish Canada as the global destination of choice for AI graduate students.
policyGap: Canada has no long-term sovereign AI compute strategy for the research community, no consistent multi-year funding guarantee for its three national AI institutes, and no structured mechanism for converting publicly funded research into domestic commercial ventures — creating a critical 'brain drain' of talent and IP to US companies and institutions.

reportTitle: Building Sovereign, Resilient AI Infrastructure for Canada
keyFindings: Canada's largest data centres currently operate at roughly 100 MW, while frontier AI data centres globally are scaling to 1 GW — a 10× gap that leaves Canada structurally unable to host leading-edge AI workloads or attract sovereign AI investment.

Canada has no comparable national framework to the EU's AI Factories initiative; despite global recognition of its AI research leadership, Canada continues to lose economic value and policy leverage by depending on foreign-controlled cloud and compute infrastructure.

The IT supply chain is globalized and largely under the control of US and Chinese hyperscalers; existing policies have allowed foreign laws with extraterritorial reach to govern Canadian data, creating unacceptable sovereignty risks for sensitive public-sector workloads.

Canada possesses significant natural advantages — cheap hydroelectric power, cold climate, abundant land, stable governance, and a skilled workforce — that should make it a globally competitive destination for AI data centres, but permitting and policy barriers have prevented realization of this potential.

Digital sovereignty does not require complete technological independence; a pragmatic 'middle-power' strategy of domestic anchor capacity plus negotiated sovereign controls from hyperscalers is achievable and would give Canada genuine bargaining power.
recommendations: Create fast-track, Canada-wide designated zones for high-capacity data centre power requirements; establish a one-window permitting office with statutory shot-clocks on processing delays to eliminate the current 18+ month approval timelines.

Establish 2–3 national compute and data campus hubs controlled by Canadian entities, seeded with public capital and operational incentives; provide capital guarantees and tax credits to encourage pension funds and large corporates to co-invest.

Launch the Canadian Compute Innovation Initiative (CCII) to build up domestic AI compute capabilities across the full stack; explore Joint Venture vehicles with private partners for a 'Strategic Adoption Cluster' serving high-sensitivity government workloads.

Negotiate the highest degree of sovereign controls offered by hyperscalers (AWS, Azure, Google Cloud) for critical government workloads; introduce legislation requiring foreign cloud partners to delegate operational authority to a Canadian entity for designated systems.

Establish Strategic Sovereign Data Trusts: select and foster high-quality, domain-specific public datasets in health, energy, natural resources, and finance; set up a federal coordinating entity to manage protected APIs for research and industry use.

Strategically enter the AI hardware supply chain: leverage Canada's mineral wealth to increase rare-earth processing capacity; pursue applied leadership in photonic and quantum computing as next-generation differentiated capabilities.
policyGap: Canada has no national framework for sovereign AI compute capacity comparable to the EU's AI Factories, no fast-track permitting process for large-scale data centres, and no legislative requirement for foreign cloud providers to maintain Canadian operational authority — leaving critical government and research workloads exposed to extraterritorial legal risk and foreign supply-chain disruption.

reportTitle: Adaptive AI Governance and Regulatory Frameworks for Canada
keyFindings: Canada ranks among the lowest globally in public trust toward AI (only 32% of Canadians believe benefits outweigh risks), while 88% support stronger governance — signalling a mandate for clear, visible regulatory action rather than voluntary principles.

The current governance landscape is fragmented: over 130 different programs, agencies, and regulatory bodies overlap without clear mandate, accountability, or speed — creating confusion for industry and inadequate protection for citizens.

Canada's proposed AIDA legislation has stalled while the EU AI Act has come into force and the US has issued 59 AI-related federal regulations in 2024 alone; the absence of enacted AI law is leaving a vacuum that erodes Canadian sovereignty and competitiveness.

High-risk AI use cases — including facial recognition in law enforcement (Clearview AI/RCMP case) and immigration decision-making (Barre v. Canada) — are already being deployed without adequate transparency, audit, or redress mechanisms.

International precedent shows effective AI governance does not require generalized legislation; targeted amendments to existing frameworks (Online Harms Act, Consumer Privacy Protection Act) combined with sector-specific risk tiers can move faster and more effectively.
recommendations: Enact a tiered AI risk framework with clear 'red lines' (unacceptable-risk AI systems prohibited), high-risk requirements (mandatory audit trails, human-in-the-loop, bias testing), and transparency obligations for limited-risk systems; align with EU AI Act categories where possible.

Establish an Independent AI Equity Commission with powers to audit public-sector AI and automated decision systems; require government-wide disclosure of all AI deployments and make audit reports publicly accessible.

Amend the Online Harms Act and Consumer Privacy Protection Act to explicitly cover AI platforms; introduce an Age-Appropriate Design Code and a 'Chatbot Safety Law' protecting minors from AI companion harms.

Mandate transparency in all AI interactions with Canadians: disclosure when AI is making or influencing decisions, right to human review for consequential decisions, and clear redress mechanisms for AI-caused harms.

Consolidate and simplify Canada's innovation support ecosystem: reduce 130+ programs to a focused, fast-moving set; appoint a National AI Coordination Office with cross-departmental authority and accountability for measurable outcomes.

Keep AI regulations 'clear and lightweight' to avoid chilling innovation while meeting safety obligations; mandate ethics and bias training for all federal AI developers and decision-makers; integrate responsible AI principles into all procurement contracts.
policyGap: Canada has no enacted national AI governance legislation, no independent AI regulator with enforcement powers, no mandatory public-sector AI disclosure regime, and no 'red lines' framework prohibiting the highest-risk AI applications — leaving Canadians without meaningful legal protection while other jurisdictions move ahead with enforceable AI law.

reportTitle: Building Safe AI Systems and Public Trust in Canada
keyFindings: AI sentiment in Canada is among the lowest globally; only 32% of Canadians believe AI benefits outweigh risks, compared to 83% in China and 80% in Indonesia — this trust deficit threatens the social licence needed for AI adoption at scale.

AI-related incidents are rising sharply globally, yet standardized responsible AI evaluations remain rare among major industrial model developers; Canada produced only 1 notable AI model in 2024 compared to 40 by the US.

Documented cases of algorithmic bias in Canadian public-sector AI (RCMP use of Clearview AI, immigration FRT misidentification of Black women in Barre v. Canada) demonstrate that high-risk AI systems are already causing material harm to marginalized communities without adequate governance.

The rapidly improving performance of AI tools makes cyberattacks faster and harder to detect; AI-based systems can identify vulnerabilities and launch attacks at a speed and scale that outpaces traditional cybersecurity defences.

Trust and safety must move at the same pace as AI innovation and adoption: if safety governance falls behind, public trust erodes; if regulation outpaces adoption, Canada loses its competitive position — both scenarios are currently playing out.
recommendations: Build a common national AI safety infrastructure: define a taxonomy of AI risks, establish a measurement framework for harm prevalence and gravity, and create mandatory reporting protocols for AI system risk profiles applicable to both public and private sector deployments.

Develop a governance model for data as a national safety asset: treat data sovereignty as inseparable from AI safety; stand up a Canadian AI Safety and Security Institute (CAISSI) that integrates safety, security, and equity mandates under one roof.

Accelerate research into AI trust and safety through CAISI (or a rebranded CAISSI); fund applied research into bias detection, explainability, and harm mitigation; require all federally funded AI models to publish safety evaluation reports.

Launch a national AI literacy and public engagement strategy: increase AI literacy across citizens, businesses, and institutions; make AI literacy part of K–12 curricula and professional certification programs; run a public 'AI Benefits' communications campaign.

Establish a national Responsible AI Playbook: require all federal AI projects to follow documented ethical codes of practice, audit trails, and bias assessments; mandate ethics training for all federal AI developers and public-sector decision-makers.

Protect minors and vulnerable populations: prohibit AI companion systems for users under 18; enact an Age-Appropriate Design Code; criminalize non-consensual sexual deepfakes; require child-first design standards for all consumer AI platforms operating in Canada.
policyGap: Canada lacks a national AI safety infrastructure — no standardized risk taxonomy, no mandatory harm-reporting regime, no independent audit capability for public-sector AI, and no enforceable prohibitions on the highest-risk AI applications targeting vulnerable populations — while AI-related incidents are rising sharply and public trust continues to fall.

reportTitle: Equitable AI Education, Skills, and Inclusion Across Canada
keyFindings: Canada ranks 44th globally for AI education and 6th in the G7 for AI adoption — the two are directly linked: AI cannot be adopted faster than the workforce's ability to understand and deploy it, and Canada's workforce AI literacy is critically low.

Only 5% of Canadian firms are actively reskilling workers for AI despite nearly half of workers fearing AI will force them to change jobs or careers; Canadian companies invest only $240 per employee per year in training, far below peer-country norms.

Indigenous Peoples face compounding barriers to AI participation: only 80.3% of First Nations communities have basic broadband access; 52% of Indigenous organizations cite privacy and security concerns as AI adoption barriers; and no unified federal funding mechanism exists for Indigenous digital transformation.

Younger workers (16–24), older workers (55–64), part-time workers, and those with lower education levels are most at risk of being left behind by AI-driven workplace change, yet targeted skills programs for these groups are absent at national scale.

Workers need legal and regulatory guardrails alongside skills investment: 85% of Canadians believe AI should be regulated by government to ensure worker protections, but Canada's AI governance framework provides no worker-specific rights or consultation requirements.
recommendations: Launch a National AI Skills Campaign targeting three simultaneous audiences: citizens (basic AI literacy), the existing workforce (applied AI skills for current roles), and technical specialists (advanced AI engineering); target at least 100,000 Canadians trained within 3 years.

Commit $5.2 billion to close the First Nations connectivity gap by 2030; dedicate a portion of the $2B Sovereign AI Compute Strategy specifically to Indigenous governments; establish a permanent funding stream for Indigenous-led AI literacy and workforce training programs.

Create a national work-integrated AI learning pathway connecting universities, colleges, polytechnics, and industry; mandate AI training for federal public servants and senior executives; incentivize employers through payroll-linked training requirements (modelled on Quebec's 1% rule).

Establish a single national 'Build with AI in Canada' front door bundling support for startups, skills programs, and talent pathways; modernize IP and data policy to retain startup value in Canada; refresh SR&ED for AI-intensive R&D including model training compute allowances.

Fund an Indigenous-led AI data sovereignty framework aligned with OCAP® principles and UNDRIP; establish clear guidelines for cloud providers and AI developers on upholding Indigenous data rights; create distinctions-based digital transformation funding streams.

Develop good-quality, nationally comparable data on employer-sponsored training and AI adoption across all sectors (private, public, broader public); update Statistics Canada tracking to include public-sector AI implementation — currently absent.
policyGap: Canada has no national AI literacy strategy, no dedicated Indigenous AI education funding stream, no mandatory employer training requirements for AI skills, and no worker-protection provisions in its AI governance framework — creating a widening skills gap that threatens both economic competitiveness and social equity as AI adoption accelerates.

reportTitle: AI Security, Digital Sovereignty, and National Defence
keyFindings: Canada faces a dual sovereignty threat: erosion of national autonomy through dependence on foreign-controlled AI developed in adversarial jurisdictions, and the risk of becoming a passive consumer of technology rather than a builder — both undermine Canada's strategic independence.

The geopolitical landscape is consolidating into a tech-bipolar world dominated by the US and China; Canada must pursue a 'middle power' strategy of building sovereign AI capability and forging coalitions with aligned nations rather than purely regulating foreign technology.

Canada's procurement culture is broken for AI security: the IDEaS program created a 'valley of death' for dual-use AI companies; Government of Canada agencies rarely purchase Canadian AI solutions first, undermining the economic and strategic case for building sovereign capability domestically.

AI is enabling a new era of cyber threats: AI-based attack systems can identify vulnerabilities and launch coordinated attacks at speeds and scales that outpace traditional defences; Canada's critical infrastructure (energy, data, communications) is inadequately secured against AI-enabled adversaries.

Canada's AI security sits on the shoulders of traditional cybersecurity — but market incentives consistently under-invest in security, and without government intervention to raise the baseline, both mature and immature Canadian organizations remain dangerously exposed.
recommendations: Forge a Middle Powers Sovereign AI Coalition: spearhead an international alliance with aligned countries (UK, Australia, Japan, Germany) to pool capital for sovereign AI infrastructure and establish mutual AI security standards, reducing individual nations' dependence on US or Chinese hyperscalers.

Establish a Canadian 'In-Q-Tel' equivalent for sovereign dual-use AI: create a dedicated strategic investment fund within BOREALIS AI (or a new entity) to make focused bets on Canadian AI companies with defence, security, and intelligence applications.

Modernize defence procurement: leverage BOREALIS to make moonshot bets on indigenous AI capabilities; adopt NATO-aligned non-lethal AI capabilities; name national AI defence champions while the market is not yet consolidated; move to an 'early adoption' procurement culture in DND.

Implement a deliberate Energy and Data Infrastructure Security Plan: protect energy grid and data centre infrastructure from AI-enabled cyberattacks; require critical infrastructure operators to maintain AI-hardened cybersecurity baselines; develop sector-specific AI security models.

Rebrand CAISI as the Canadian AI Safety and Security Institute (CAISSI); integrate CSE, NRC, and Statistics Canada expertise; deliver an ambitious AI safety and security implementation plan focused on agentic AI risks; establish a phased roadmap for sovereignty in Canadian government systems.

Create a Fast Track Immigration Status Program for leading AI scientists and dual-use AI talent currently in the US or allied nations; build a national talent circulation model ensuring public-sector AI expertise serves Canadian interests rather than leaking to foreign entities.
policyGap: Canada has no dedicated sovereign AI security investment fund, no modernized defence AI procurement framework, no mandatory security requirements for critical AI infrastructure operators, and no formal process for designating national AI security champions — leaving Canada strategically exposed as adversaries accelerate AI-enabled capabilities against which Canada currently has limited sovereign defence capacity.

reportTitle: Canada's International AI Positioning and Collaborative Strategy
keyFindings: Canada is positioned between two AI superpowers (US and China) engaged in increasingly coercive economic behaviour; Canada's historical dependence on the US market and US tech infrastructure creates an acute strategic vulnerability that demands proactive mitigation.

Canada's trusted national brand, multilingual culture, stable rule of law, and strong research institutions provide genuine comparative advantages in specialized AI use cases (health data, climate, natural resources, multilingual AI) that neither the US nor China can easily replicate.

Canada's G7 presidency in 2025 presents a unique and time-limited opportunity to translate international commitments — such as the G7 Leaders' Statement on AI — into binding multilateral governance frameworks and shared sovereign AI infrastructure compacts.

International counterparts have moved faster: the EU has enacted the AI Act and launched AI Factories; Singapore has published Industry Digital Plans for AI; the UK has integrated AI into public service delivery at scale — Canada risks losing its position as a global AI governance leader.

Regulating foreign technologies by default is a losing strategy; Canada must build companies with Canadian values and export them to the world, establishing economic and reputational leadership rather than attempting to constrain foreign AI after the fact.
recommendations: Use Canada's G7 presidency to lead on multilateral AI governance: push for international AI risk taxonomies, shared safety evaluation standards, and mutual recognition of responsible AI certifications to reduce regulatory fragmentation for Canadian exporters.

Spearhead a Middle Powers Sovereign AI Coalition with aligned democracies (UK, Australia, Japan, Germany, Netherlands) to pool compute investments, share safety research, and establish common standards that counter US/China platform dominance.

Pursue 'compete, collaborate, defer' international research strategy: compete in foundational research and Canadian-strength specialties; collaborate on frontier-scale foundation models with trusted allies; defer costly general-purpose LLM arms races that don't advance sovereignty.

Reproduce and improve upon Singapore's Industry Digital Plans for AI: publish sector-specific AI adoption roadmaps, standards, and incentives for priority Canadian industries (mining, energy, health, finance) to signal predictable, export-oriented policy to international partners.

Export Canadian AI values: position Canada's stability, diversity, inclusion, and democratic governance as export advantages in military AI (NATO, EU, APAC), international development, and trusted AI platform markets — especially as trust in US and Chinese AI erodes.

Ensure Canadian firms and experts shape international regulatory approaches as they take firmer form; mandate active participation of Canadian AI institutes in OECD, ISO/IEC, and ITU AI standards processes to protect Canadian commercial and policy interests.
policyGap: Canada has no coherent international AI strategy beyond individual bilateral relationships; no formal multilateral sovereign AI coalition; no structured approach to exporting Canadian AI governance values; and no mechanism to leverage Canada's G7 presidency for binding international AI safety commitments — leaving Canada's global AI positioning to chance at a critical moment of international standard-setting.
